Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I had to work around a lot of other responsibilities. I usually tried to block out time on weekends when things were quieter, and I'd download everything I needed from the LMS in one go. Being a visual learner, I'd print out diagrams and charts when I could afford the printing costs, then study from those throughout the week. It wasn't the most organized system, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and what was happening in my life outside of school. Some weeks I could barely touch anything because of work or family obligations, then I'd have to cram everything into a few intense sessions. I tried to keep some consistency on Sunday evenings, but honestly, that fell apart more often than not. When assignments were due, that's when I really buckled down regardless of what else was going on.
Q3: Usually it was an assignment deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about new materials or an upcoming due date, and that would push me to check in. I didn't log in just to browse around or see what was new because my internet access isn't always reliable and I needed to be intentional about when I used it. Every login had a purpose, usually downloading what I needed or submitting work.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a daily habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, especially at the start of a new module, but it was more of a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my routine. What did become routine was my offline study sessions with downloaded materials. The LMS was like a supply depot I'd visit periodically rather than a place I lived in.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me because I could download them and watch them multiple times at my own pace. Visual content like diagrams, flowcharts, and presentation slides really stuck with me better than walls of text. I also appreciated when instructors posted summary documents because I could print those out and annotate them. Anything I could take offline and study visually was gold for me.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums because by the time I logged in, conversations had usually moved on and I felt like I'd missed the moment. Some of the reading materials that were just dense text without any visual aids, I'd skim those pretty quickly or skip them if I felt I understood the concept from the videos. It wasn't that I thought they were useless, just that my time and energy were limited, so I had to prioritize what worked best for how I learn.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to either an assignment or the exam. If I watched a video and immediately understood something that had confused me, that felt valuable. I also valued anything that I could take with me and review later without needing to be online. Honestly, with limited resources and time, I had to be strategic about what I invested my energy into.
Q8: Optional activities often felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on. Required activities had clear consequences if I didn't do them, so they automatically got priority. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional stuff because I knew it could help, but when you're juggling as much as I am, you have to make hard choices. The optional activities felt like they were designed for students with more time and fewer pressures.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture videos, save PDFs, and screenshot important diagrams to study from my phone when I didn't have internet. I also used YouTube videos on similar topics when I needed different explanations of concepts. My notebook was full of hand-drawn diagrams and summaries that I'd review during breaks at work or on the bus. The LMS was just the starting point.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but it wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams without touching the LMS. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a cousin who studied something similar, working through problems together on paper. My real learning often happened in quiet moments away from any screen, just me and my notes.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in once, download everything available for that module, and then work through it all offline over the following days or weeks. A single video might get watched three or four times from my downloads, but the LMS would only show one view. I had to be efficient with my internet access, so batch downloading became my strategy for staying on top of the material.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really difficult, usually when financial stress peaked or family responsibilities demanded more of my attention. Feeling sad and overwhelmed made it hard to concentrate even when I had time to study. During those times, I'd fall behind on logging in, though I tried to keep up with assignments because I knew my grades depended on them. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me push through some of those darker moments.
Q13: All of those factors affected my LMS use significantly. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log in with good intentions but find myself just staring at the screen. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules until I absolutely had to face them. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between studying and other urgent needs. I learned to work in short, focused bursts when I could because long study sessions just weren't realistic for my life.
Q14: There were times when I'd have the LMS open but my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or family stuff. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything, just going through the motions. Sometimes fatigue made it impossible to focus even though I was technically looking at course content. Those sessions felt wasted, but I kept trying because giving up wasn't an option for me.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion forum participation because it felt less urgent than assignments with hard deadlines. Sometimes I'd avoid topics that confused me, hoping they'd make more sense later or wouldn't be heavily tested. I knew this wasn't smart, but when you're already stressed and struggling, facing something difficult feels overwhelming. I'd tell myself I'd get to it when I had more energy, but that energy didn't always come.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in, download materials, and get to work. My activity probably looked very spiky, with quiet periods followed by bursts of engagement right before due dates. It's not ideal, but deadlines gave me the structure I needed when everything else in my life felt chaotic.
Q17: When I got feedback that showed I was on the right track, it motivated me to keep pushing even when things were hard. Positive comments on assignments reminded me that my efforts were paying off despite all my challenges. Critical feedback sometimes stung, but I tried to use it constructively because I couldn't afford to make the same mistakes twice. Feedback was like a compass that helped me know where to focus my limited study time.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my downloads and study sessions around my other commitments. Having content organized by topic made it easier to focus on one thing at a time rather than feeling overwhelmed by everything at once. However, the expectation of regular attendance and participation was difficult given my situation. I appreciated when the structure allowed some flexibility in how I engaged with materials.
Q19: Having video content available for download made engagement much easier for someone in my situation with unreliable internet. Clear assignment rubrics helped me understand exactly what was expected without having to guess. What made it harder was when important information was only shared in live sessions I couldn't attend, or when discussions moved too fast for me to participate meaningfully. More asynchronous options would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to find it, so they keep clicking through materials hoping something will click. Others might be dealing with anxiety or stress that makes them feel like they need to constantly check the LMS without being able to focus on actual work. It could also be technical issues or confusion about what's actually required. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually describes my experience pretty well. My LMS activity was relatively low because I did most of my studying offline with downloaded materials. But I still managed to earn a Merit because I was genuinely engaging with the content, just not in ways the system could track. I think it shows that some students have found efficient ways to learn that don't require constant online presence. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students are most active and which resources get accessed most frequently. They can track submission rates and basic participation metrics. But they miss the depth of understanding, the offline study hours, the conversations with peers outside the platform, and the personal struggles students overcome to stay engaged. They see the surface but not the substance of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just passively consuming content. It means caring about understanding, not just passing. For me, engagement looks like drawing diagrams until a concept clicks, reviewing my notes on the bus, and pushing through even when I'm tired and sad because I believe this education will change my circumstances. It's about commitment to learning despite obstacles.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how many hours I spent studying from printed materials or downloaded videos. It wouldn't capture the emotional effort it takes to stay motivated when you're dealing with financial stress and sadness. It wouldn't show the resilience required to keep going when attendance is impossible and resources are limited. My journey through this course involved a lot of invisible struggle and determination that no log file could ever record.
